our brains have to be memory efficient and so we've split into two selves: the remembering self and the experiencing self

the person you think you are and make decisions as is the remembering self
the person who experiences such decisions is the experiencing self

the remembering self parses the experiencing self experiences and condenses those by averaging or by peak-end analysis (peak value and the end value of the experience). 
This mean that things as duration are disregarded, something that your experiencing self will suffer through

experiment done by Daniel Kahneman: 
subjects were asked to immerse their hands in ice water for 60 seconds and then asked to do those 60 seconds + 30 seconds were they would increase the temperature gradually.

when asked which one would they rather repeat (60 or 90 secs trial), the majority picked the 90 secs
the sum of the pain of the experience was worse in the 90 seconds trial
however, the average pain was better

the narratives everyone likes follow this pattern of experience condensation. stories that have low endings will always be worse than with high endings. even though, if you sum the whole experience, one can be more enjoyable than the other despite the ending.
